coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multi-peak emitting carbon dots using phenylenediamine isomers

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Baohui
Song, Tao
Cheng, Fang
Ren, Lingling
Jia, Zhili
Song, Lingyu
Pu, Chen
coredata.dc:description: Revealing the relationship between structure and optical properties of multi-peak emissive carbon dots (CDs) is important. Three types of multi-peak emission CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with phenylenediamine (PD) isomers (o/m/p) and maleic anhydride serving as precursors. During the synthesis process, three PDs exhibited different microstructural and chemical states, resulting in various fluorescence emission centers. The multi-peak emission behavior of o-CDs and m-CDs is composed of molecular fluorophores and carbon core states, while that of p-CDs consists of surface states and carbon core states. These different fluorescence centers lead to diverse fluorescence behaviors of the CDs in different solvents and environments. This work inspires the clarification of the relationship between the structural and optical properties of multi-peak emissive CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-peak emitting
Photoluminescent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and corresponding size distribution histograms (insets) of (a-c) o/m/p-CDs, respectively. (d–i) High-resolution XPS spectra (C 1 S/N 1 s/O 1 s) of o/m/p-CDs, respectively.
UV–visible absorption spectra of (a-c) o/m/p-CDs, respectively. PL spectrum of (d-f) o/m/p-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

PL spectrum of (a-c) o/m/p-CDs in water and strong acid solvent. PLE spectrum of (e-f) o/m/p-CDs at different emission wavelengths. (g) UV–visible absorption spectra of p-CDs in water and ethanol. PL spectrum of p-CDs in (h) water and (i) ethanol.
Synthesis and fluorescence mechanism diagram of o/m/p-CDs, respectively.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multi-peak emitting carbon dots using phenylenediamine isomers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Magnetism Key Laboratory of Zhejiang Province, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Magnetism Key Laboratory of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Magnetism Key Laboratory of Zhejiang Province, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Innovation Center of Graphene Metrology and Standardization for State Market Regulation, National Institute of Metrology, Beijing 100013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Innovation Center of Graphene Metrology and Standardization for State Market Regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Innovation Center of Graphene Metrology and Standardization for State Market Regulation, National Institute of Metrology, Beijing 100013, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Baohui Li and Tao Song contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Revealing the relationship between structure and optical properties of multi-peak emissive carbon dots (CDs) is important. Three types of multi-peak emission CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with phenylenediamine (PD) isomers (o/m/p) and maleic anhydride serving as precursors. During the synthesis process, three PDs exhibited different microstructural and chemical states, resulting in various fluorescence emission centers. The multi-peak emission behavior of o-CDs and m-CDs is composed of molecular fluorophores and carbon core states, while that of p-CDs consists of surface states and carbon core states. These different fluorescence centers lead to diverse fluorescence behaviors of the CDs in different solvents and environments. This work inspires the clarification of the relationship between the structural and optical properties of multi-peak emissive CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess potential in varied applications. For the case of CDs in luminescent imaging [1], however, the application is greatly limited due to single blue or green fluorescence. Previous studies have shown that heteroatom doping such as N, S, B, and P can introduce certain functional groups into CDs and affect electronic structure. Either the overlap of atomic orbitals between heteroatoms and carbon atoms, or the interactions among atoms, would influence the electronic structure, nanostructure, and chemical arrangements of CDs, thus modulating the photoluminescence performance [2]. PD, the simplest aromatic diamine, not only shows a conjugate structure based on the benzene ring, but also has diversely substituted positions of amino, making it a suitable candidate for N-doped CDs. Xu et al. used three isomers of PD as nitrogen and carbon sources and prepared three fluorescent CDs through a one-step solvothermal method [3]. In contrast, p-CDs exhibited a multi-peak emission which differed from the single emission of o-CDs and m-CDs. However, the reason PD isomers produce multi-peak emission remains unclear, making it important to discuss the cause of this phenomenon.

As a novel and important phenomenon, CDs have attracted considerable attention by multi-peak emission characteristics. This study constructed three distinct fluorescence systems with multi-peak emission using PD isomers and maleic anhydride (MAH) as raw materials. PD and MAH are easy to dehydrate under mild temperature conditions to form amide derivatives, and MAH can provide conjugated structures and hydrophilic groups for CDs. Based on the characterization of morphology, structure and fluorescence characteristics, the photoluminescent (PL) mechanism of multi-peak emission CDs synthesized by different precursors was elucidated.

Three batches of isomers of phenylenediamine (m-PD, o-PD, and p-PD, 0.2 g each) and 0.2 g maleic anhydride were dissolved into 20 ml pure water, followed by ultrasound and then transferred to the reactor, and reacted at 180℃ for 4 h. The final products were dialyzed for 24 h and denoted as o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs, respectively.

Fig. 1 (a–c) show uniform distribution of grain size as 3.88, 4.78, and 2.46 nm for o/m/ p-CDs, respectively. The illustration demonstrates a lattice d-spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the graphitic carbon (100) lattice plane [4]. The supplementary Raman image in Fig. S1 reflects different graphitization degrees of synthetic CDs, and the intensity ratio of D band and G band peaks of o-PD, m-PD are 1.86, 0.82, and 2.19, respectively. Increased CDs’ graphitization leads to higher carbon core density and smaller particle sizes.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S2) shows a gradual decrease in N content from m-CDs to o-CDs and p-CDs, inversely correlating with graphitization, indicating a loss of amino groups during this process. Fig. 1 (d–l) displays high-resolution XPS spectra for the C, N, and O elements. All CDs exhibit similar bonding types, implying comparable graphitized structures. The three Gaussian peaks at 284.8, 286.0, and 288.8 eV are assigned to C = C/C–C, C-O-C and O-C = O. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 1e, 1 h, 1 k) of o-CDs indicate the formation of pyrrolic N (400.3 eV), while m-CDs and p-CDs show that of pyridine N (398.9 eV) [5].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S3) indicate that o-CDs and m-CDs exhibit strong absorption in the amide I (1667 cm−1) and amide II (1563 cm−1) bands [6]. In contrast, p-CDs show decreased surface functional group strength, with the strongest absorption peak at C = C (1510 cm−1) and stretching vibrations for C = N (1625 cm−1) and C = O (1710 cm−1). It can be inferred that an amidation reaction occur between PD and MAH, in which p-CDs amide is further reacted to achieve higher carbonization. Hence the precursor reaction site will produce structural differences in CDs.

As shown in the UV–visible absorption spectra of o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs in Fig. 2 (a-c), the absorption of 250–280 nm comes from π-π* transition of C = C and C = N bonds in the aromatic ring structure [7]. The absorption of 280–400 nm comes from the defect state of CDs and n-π* transition caused by surface functional groups [8]. P-CDs remove plenty of surface functional groups during the synthesis process, which causes the peak value in the n-π* region to be significantly weaker than that of o-CDs and m-CDs.

The PL spectra in Fig. 2 (d-f) show that CDs exhibit multi-peak emission. Both o-CDs and m-CDs have a stable peak at 425 nm and a tunable peak that shifts from blue to green with increasing excitation wavelength. P-CDs display multiple emission peaks shifting from 350 nm to 600 nm, attributed to different fluorescence centers in the CDs. In general, tunable peak is caused by the quantum confined effect of the carbon core state [9], and table peak is caused by molecular fluorescence. Figure S4 shows the fitted PL lifetime curves of CDs at different emission wavelengths. The distinct PL lifetimes at different emission wavelengths suggest that the emission peaks of multi-peak fluorescent CDs arise from distinct fluorescence centers (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solution environment on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were dispersed in a strong acid solvent (pH = 0.26) to explore the different fluorescence centers, as shown in Fig. 3 (a–c). Under the excitation wavelength of 375 nm, o-CDs exhibited a wide emission spectrum, with peak fitting revealing 425 nm (type 1) and 460 nm (type 2). Under acidic environment, type 1 disappears, and type 2 intensity decreases, type 1 is destroyed and unable to produce 425 nm fluorescence resulting in type 2 fluorescence. Because the absorption region of type 2 is exactly the emission region of type 1 (Fig. 3(d)).

Similarly, m-CDs showed two fluorescence centers at 425 nm (type 1) and 460 nm (type 2) by Gaussian fitting. Under strong acid, the type 1 is completely quenched, akin to o-CDs (molecular state fluorescence). Type 2 is not affected. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum (Fig. 3(e)) indicates no self-absorption of m-CDs, and type 2 shows red-shifts with the movement of excitation luminescence, which suggests that the fluorescence center is carbon core state.

The acidic environment didn't significantly change p-CDs' peak shape, designing an experiment on solvent effects. P-CDs exhibit different fluorescence colors in solutions of varying polarities. Water and ethanol were used to compare fluorescence changes at different wavelengths (Fig. 3(h–i)). The strongest PL peak shifted from the original 550 nm to 450 nm. Under 400 nm excitation, the fluorescence was a combination of 440 nm (type 1) and 500 nm (type 2). Type 2 has the characteristic that the carbon core state is redshifted with excitation wavelength. In the UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 3(g)), the n-π* transition representing the surface state at 300 nm is enhanced when the polarity changes, and the fluorescence intensity of type 1 is significantly enhanced.

Michael's model of carbon core, molecular, and surface states [10] can explain the multi-peak emission behavior of CDs (Fig. 4 ). When o-CDs are excited by 375 nm, the molecular state (type 1) emits 425 nm fluorescence after partial relaxation. Since 375 nm wavelength light failed to activate carbon core (type 2) PL channels due to the lack of an absorption peak, 425 nm fluorescence was reabsorbed, and 460 nm fluorescence was emitted after the energy rearrangement.

In m-CDs, without reabsorption, the molecular state transited (type 1) from the ground to excited state under excitation light, relaxed, and emitted 425 nm fluorescence. Some other energy transitions to the carbon core state (type 2) before returning to the ground state.

In ethanol, ultraviolet light excites p-CDs to a higher energy state. Some energy relaxed back to the ground state via the surface state, while another portion transited to an excited state associated with the carbon core state before relaxing back to the ground state. However, in water, transitions involving surface states are quenched due to energy resonance.

Using PD isomer as a precursor to react with MAH in hydrothermal environment, three kinds of multi-peak emitting CDs with different fluorescence properties were obtained. The multi-peak emission behavior of the three types of carbon dots is caused by different fluorescence centers, o-CDs and m-CDs are due to molecular fluorophores and carbon core states, and p-CDs are due to surface states and carbon core states. Based on the performance of CDs in different environments, they showed potential application value as anti-counterfeiting agents.

Baohui Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Tao Song: Writing – review & editing. Fang Cheng: Writing – review & editing. Lingling Ren: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology. Zhili Jia: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Lingyu Song: Data curation. Chen Pu: Methodology, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic Research Program Fund of National Institute of Metrology, China (AKYZD2312).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.137693.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unveiling Unconventional Luminescence Behavior of Multicolor Carbon Dots Derived from Phenylenediamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yan, J. Li, X. Zhao, X. Gong, Unveiling Unconventional Luminescence Behavior of Multicolor Carbon Dots Derived from Phenylenediamine, J Phys Chem Lett 14(26) (2023) 5975-5984.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: pH and solvent induced discoloration behavior of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots
Coll. Surf. A: Physicochem. Eng. Asp.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Regulation of multi-color fluorescence of carbonized polymer dots by multiple contributions of effective conjugate size, surface state, and molecular fluorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wan, X. Zhang, Y. Jiang, S. Xu, J. Li, M. Yu, K. Zhang, Z. Su, Regulation of multi-color fluorescence of carbonized polymer dots by multiple contributions of effective conjugate size, surface state, and molecular fluorescence, J Mater Chem B 10(36) (2022) 6991-7002.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Hola, M. Sudolska, S. Kalytchuk, D. Nachtigallova, A.L. Rogach, M. Otyepka, R. Zboril, Graphitic Nitrogen Triggers Red Fluorescence in Carbon Dots, ACS Nano 11(12) (2017) 12402-12410.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Dots: A Unique Fluorescent Cocktail of Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Fu, F. Ehrat, Y. Wang, K.Z. Milowska, C. Reckmeier, A.L. Rogach, J.K. Stolarczyk, A.S. Urban, J. Feldmann, Carbon Dots: A Unique Fluorescent Cocktail of Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons, Nano Lett 15(9) (2015) 6030-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Communication of molecular fluorophores with other photoluminescence centres in carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Langer, L. Zdrazil, M. Medved, M. Otyepka, Communication of molecular fluorophores with other photoluminescence centres in carbon dots, Nanoscale 15(8) (2023) 4022-4032.
